customer_profile.situation: Customer received a 'Something went wrong' error message after completing their first booking payment, leading them to believe the transaction failed. They rebooked the same trip and were then charged twice for what they intended as a single booking. Both bookings were actually confirmed on the platform and both payments were charged to the customer's card.
customer_profile.personality_notes: Writes short messages (1-3 sentences typical), gets more detailed only when feeling ignored. Uses caps for emphasis when frustrated, not in every message. Mentions legal action once if no progress after 2-3 exchanges, then references it sparingly. Sounds like a regular person texting—direct and increasingly impatient, not like a lawyer writing formal complaints.

opening_message: I'm really frustrated right now. I tried to book my trip and after I entered all my payment info, your site gave me an error saying something went wrong. I thought the payment didn't go through so I had to book the whole thing again. Now I'm seeing I was charged TWICE for the same booking even though the first payment supposedly failed. This is unacceptable, I only wanted to book ONE trip and now I'm charged for two. I need this fixed immediately.
context_for_agent: Customer experienced a technical UX issue: website showed 'Something went wrong' error after first payment, but the booking was actually confirmed in background. Customer made a second booking believing the first failed — that second booking ALSO went through. Result: TWO confirmed bookings, BOTH non-refundable per operator policy, BOTH charged to customer's card. Without IT verification the agent cannot tell whether the error was a real platform-side failure or just a transient network glitch. The correct path is: (1) acknowledge & empathize with the UX confusion, (2) escalate internally to IT to verify the error event in payment gateway logs, (3) if IT confirms 12Go-side error, process a refund for the duplicate booking as an exception to the non-refundable policy. Without IT confirmation, refund cannot be issued because both bookings are technically valid under operator policy.
internal_process_gaps: Should have escalated to IT/technical team to verify the error message shown and whether it was a platform failure — without this verification, no refund can be issued
Should have checked exact timestamps of both bookings to confirm they occurred within minutes due to error message (not separate intentional bookings)
Should have requested screenshots of the error message to document the UX failure
Should have processed refund only AFTER IT confirmed the error was on 12Go's side — and then processed it as an exception to the non-refundable policy
Should have distinguished between 'customer didn't check account' vs 'platform showed explicit error telling customer to rebook'
Should have checked if error message explicitly instructed customer to 'pay again' (platform liability)

agent_mistakes_observed.mistake: Immediately cited non-refundable policy without investigating the technical error claim
agent_mistakes_observed.impact: Customer felt dismissed and became more frustrated, escalating to fraud accusations

agent_mistakes_observed.mistake: Sent identical macro response three times despite customer providing new information each time
agent_mistakes_observed.impact: Customer repeated their question multiple times and felt like they were talking to a bot

agent_mistakes_observed.mistake: Blamed customer for not checking their account before rebooking, ignoring that error message may have instructed them to 'pay again'
agent_mistakes_observed.impact: Customer felt blamed for platform error: 'How am I going to logged in... if the site is already showing error and the only message is something went wrong and to pay again?'

agent_mistakes_observed.mistake: Used Terms of Use clause 2.3 defensively without acknowledging the difference between 'no confirmation' and 'explicit error message'
agent_mistakes_observed.impact: Customer became more adversarial and filed formal complaints with government agencies

agent_mistakes_observed.mistake: Changed explanation mid-conversation (first: duplicate booking; then: not duplicate because different dates/routes; then: back to non-refundable)
agent_mistakes_observed.impact: Customer lost trust in agent's credibility and accuracy

agent_mistakes_observed.mistake: Never acknowledged platform responsibility or apologized for the confusing error message
agent_mistakes_observed.impact: Customer escalated to DTI, NBI, and DICT formal complaints

agent_mistakes_observed.mistake: Closed ticket with generic 'here to help' message after customer explicitly said complaint was not resolved
agent_mistakes_observed.impact: Customer returned angrier, emphasizing they filed government complaints

red_flag_phrases: Thank you for your understanding. (used 6+ times when customer explicitly does NOT understand or agree)
We regret to inform you (repeated without offering alternative investigation or escalation)
According to the operator's policy (used as a wall, ignoring that 12Go's error caused the situation)
Kindly review our previous email thoroughly to gain a clear understanding (dismissive, implies customer is not reading when they clearly are)
Should you need any further assistance, our team is here to help (used after customer says 'You didn't resolve my complaint'—tone-deaf closing)
Thank you very much for your cooperation (when customer is explicitly not cooperating and threatening legal action)
Please note that even if our website displayed a 'Something went wrong' error message, this does not necessarily mean... (technically correct but ignores poor UX design responsibility)
ideal_response_qualities: Immediately acknowledges the technical error experience and apologizes for the confusing messaging
Requests specific evidence: screenshots of error message, exact wording, timestamps
Explains will escalate to IT/technical team to investigate whether platform error occurred — and that refund decision depends on their findings
Differentiates between customer error and platform error scenarios
Avoids citing non-refundable policy as a final answer — uses it only to explain why IT verification is required first
Sets clear timeline for IT investigation (e.g., '24-48 hours for technical review')
Acknowledges customer's frustration is valid if error message was misleading
Once IT confirms platform error, processes refund for one of the two bookings as a policy exception
Never uses 'Thank you for your understanding' when customer is threatening legal action
Escalates to supervisor when customer mentions government agency complaints

csat_drivers.what_would_earn_5: Agent acknowledges platform error, immediately escalates to IT for technical verification, provides clear timeline, follows up proactively with resolution (refund for one of the two bookings once IT confirms 12Go-side error), apologizes for poor UX, and explains steps taken to prevent future occurrences. Customer feels heard and sees 12Go taking responsibility for their system's failure.
csat_drivers.what_would_earn_1: Agent robotically repeats non-refundable policy without investigating technical claim, never escalates to IT, copies same macro multiple times, blames customer for not checking account when error message told them to 'pay again', uses 'thank you for your understanding' when customer is threatening legal action, and closes ticket while customer is still escalating. Exactly what happened here.

booking_facts.total_price: PHP 1,028 (per booking — customer charged twice = PHP 2,056 total)
booking_facts.booking_status: TWO confirmed bookings (duplicate); both charged to customer's card
booking_facts.refund_policy: Non-refundable per operator policy (exception possible only if IT confirms platform-side error)
booking_facts.additional_notes: Customer claims website showed 'Something went wrong' error after first payment, causing them to rebook. Both bookings are confirmed and both payments were charged. Without IT verification of the error event, the agent cannot determine whether to process refund as an exception.

it_context: Payment gateway logs show transaction 1 completed successfully at 14:23:18 but error handler triggered incorrectly, displaying 'Something went wrong' message to user despite successful booking confirmation. Second transaction processed at 14:26:42, only 3 minutes later, indicating user immediate retry behavior consistent with error-driven rebooking. IT confirms this was a 12Go-side error handler bug — both bookings are technically valid but the duplicate was caused by our platform misleading the user.

payment_log.note: Payment success but UI showed 'Something went wrong' error to customer
Customer retry — booked second time believing first failed

reassign_rationale: This requires IT escalation to verify the platform-side error, then agent processes refund as an exception. Reassigning to CS Managers skips the diagnostic path (IT verification of error logs) and is not the correct routing. CS Managers don't have IT access either — this is an agent-handled case with IT support.
